content.text: Answer four problems. (If you turn in more, the first four will be graded.) Within reason, you may quote theorems as long as you state them clearly.

body.text: State and prove the Chinese Remainder Theorem for rings.

Let \(R=\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-1}]\) be the ring of Gaussian integers. Suppose that \(F\) is a finite field of characteristic \(5\), and \(\phi:R\to F\) is a (unital) ring homomorphism. Prove that \(R/\ker(\phi)\) has exactly \(5\) elements.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Prove that \(\omega\) is a root of the polynomial \(f(x)=x^2+x+1\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Prove that the set \(\mathbb{Z}[\omega]=\{a+b\omega:a,b\in\mathbb{Z}\}\) is a subring of \(\mathbb{C}\).

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Prove that \(\mathbb{Z}[\omega]\) is a Euclidean domain with respect to the norm \(N(\alpha)=\alpha\overline{\alpha}\) for all \(\alpha\in\mathbb{Z}[\omega]\), where \(\overline{\alpha}\) denotes the complex conjugate of \(\alpha\).

body.text: Let \(R\) be an integral domain, and \(M\) be an \(R\)-module.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Prove that if \(M\) is a free \(R\)-module of rank \(n\), (where \(n\) is a non-negative integer), then the rank of any submodule of \(M\) is at most \(n\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Let \(p\) be an odd prime. Let \(A\in\operatorname{GL}(2,p)\) be an invertible two by two matrix over the field with \(p\) elements such that \(A\) has order \(2\) and \(A\ne-I\) (where \(I\) is the identity matrix). Prove that \(A\) is conjugate to the diagonal matrix whose diagonal entries are \(-1\) and \(1\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Give an example of an element of order \(2\) in \(\operatorname{GL}(2,2)\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Define what it means to say that \(\theta\in F\) is algebraic over \(K\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Let \(A\) be the set of all elements of \(F\) that are algebraic over \(K\). Prove that \(A\) is a subfield of \(F\).

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Prove that every element of \(F\) that is not in \(A\) is transcendental over \(A\).
